nativeRange.habitat: Rubble areas near or within coral reefs, coastal sand slopes, and deep lagoons; typically found at 5–40 m depth in symbiotic association with alpheid pistol shrimps, most often Alpheus ochrostriatus.

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate at least 5–8 cm deep for burrow construction
loose coral rubble or flat rocks near the sand for burrow anchoring
open sandy area in the foreground for observation
low-flow zone near the burrow entrance

diet.notes: Carnivorous; generally accepts a variety of fresh or frozen meaty foods including mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped marine seafood, and small carnivore pellets. Twice-daily feeding in small portions is commonly advised. Has been reported to be fairly easy to feed in captivity compared to other shrimp gobies.

goodWith: pistol shrimp (Alpheus ochrostriatus — natural symbiotic partner)
small peaceful reef fish (firefish, cardinalfish, royal grammas)
avoidWith: large aggressive predators (groupers, lionfish, large angelfish)
boisterous bottom-dwellers that disrupt the burrow
triggerfish and crustacean-hunting species (will eat the partner shrimp)
other shrimp gobies competing for the same sandy territory

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe; 1669x1252. Most striking, vividly coloured individual: whitish body with deep reddish-brown bars, yellow saddles, iridescent blue spots and orange head spots with orange eye-ring all sharply shown on a vibrant live fish. Whole animal in frame.
Rickard Zerpe; 2403x1802. Clean classic sentry pose propped on rock with fins spread; whole fish sharp, reddish-brown bars and orange/blue head spots clear. Slight green water cast.
Emoke Denes; 4322x2881. Aquarium display shot showing full fish on sand as hobbyists see it; whitish body, red bars and red head spots visible. Head crisp, body slightly soft.

summary: An Indo-Pacific shrimp goby reaching around 10 cm, recognized by its whitish body with reddish-brown bars and blue spots. Wheeler's Shrimp Goby forms a well-documented symbiotic burrow partnership with pistol shrimps and is generally considered reef-safe.
careNotes:
Wheeler's Shrimp Goby is one of the larger Amblyeleotris shrimp gobies in the hobby, also widely sold under the name Gorgeous Prawn-Goby. Like other members of its genus, it naturally pairs with a pistol shrimp — most often Alpheus ochrostriatus in the wild — which excavates and maintains a burrow while the goby stands sentry and signals danger with fin flicks. This mutualistic relationship tends to be well-expressed in captivity when a compatible pistol shrimp is provided.

A fine sand bed of at least 5–8 cm and some coral rubble or flat rocks for burrow anchoring are generally advised to support this behaviour. An open sandy foreground area helps with observation. A tight-fitting lid is important, as gobies of this genus have been known to jump when startled.

Because Wheeler's Shrimp Goby is a larger, territorial bottom-dweller, it needs adequate sandy footprint and a stable shared burrow territory; a tank of at least 100 litres is recommended to give the goby and its shrimp partner room to establish themselves comfortably. Some sources suggest that conspecific pairs may become established in sufficiently spacious tanks but may quarrel in smaller quarters.

The species is generally noted as fairly hardy and disease-resistant. It has been reported to have been successfully spawned in captivity.
breedingNotes: Has been reported to have been successfully spawned in captivity. Eggs are typically deposited inside the shared burrow and the male tends the clutch. Rearing pelagic larvae is the main challenge, generally requiring live rotifers and copepod nauplii. Detailed breeding protocols are not widely documented in the hobby literature.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species product page on LiveAquaria. Primary source for hobby water parameters, minimum tank size, temperament, diet, care level, and reef safety. The assigned manifest URL (gobies collection page) did not list the species directly; the product page was reached via on-site search.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Gobiidae, order Gobiiformes), full native range (East Africa to Fiji, Japan to Great Barrier Reef), maximum standard length (10.0 cm SL), wild depth range (5–40 m), wild temperature (21–30°C; preferred 25.5–29.3°C), habitat type (rubble/reef), and symbiotic partner species (Alpheus ochrostriatus). Temperature ceiling from FishBase (30°C) drove the averaged upper bound upward from LiveAquaria's 26°C to 28°C. Substituted as the confirmed secondary after Reef2Reef thread (403 Forbidden) and Reef2Reef wiki (403 Forbidden) were inaccessible.

fish.breedingNotes: Reported to have been bred in captivity; rearing pelagic larvae requires live small foods (rotifers, copepod nauplii) and is the primary challenge for hobbyist attempts.
